section: Enhancing strength and ductility in Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C austenitic low-density steels through Ni and Cr alloying synergy adding

context_update.summary: This chunk includes the abstract and introduction. It presents the developed steel composition Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C (wt%) alloyed with Ni and Cr, produced via thermo-mechanical processing with ultra-fast cooling. The steel aims for density below 6.8 g/cm³ and achieves YS >1100 MPa, UTS >1200 MPa, and TEL >45%. Key strengthening mechanisms are grain boundary reinforcement, solid solution hardening, precipitation strengthening from nanoscale κ-carbides and LRO domains, and secondary slip activation. Ni and Cr are reported to modulate κ-carbide precipitation thresholds; Cr specifically promotes high-density dislocation arrays (HDDA) and dynamic slip band refinement (DSBR) to enhance strain hardenability. Introduction defines κ-carbide as E2₁ perovskite (Fe,Mn)₃AlC, κ′ as (Fe,Mn)₃AlCₓ (x<1) L′1₂ metastable phase, and L1₂ ordered γ-austenite. It reviews that 1 wt% Al reduces density by 1.3%, Cr mitigates intergranular κ*-carbide precipitation, and previous austenitic lightweight steels have YS 300–800 MPa and density 6.8–7.4 g/cm³. Deformation substructure evolution (Taylor lattice to dislocation walls) and SIP effect are also mentioned. This context sets the stage for experimental details, microstructure analysis, and mechanical property discussions in subsequent chunks.
material_systems: Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C austenitic steel with Ni and Cr
mechanisms: precipitation strengthening from κ-carbides
solid solution hardening
grain boundary reinforcement
secondary slip activation
HDDA and DSBR by Cr

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the experimental procedures and initial microstructure characterization. Four Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C steels alloyed with different Ni and Cr levels (3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr, 2Ni5Cr) were cast, homogenized, hot-rolled with 95% reduction to 4 mm sheets, and ultrafast cooled at ~40 °C/s. Tensile specimens were machined along RD and tested at 3 mm/min with interrupted strains of 3/5% and 10%. EBSD and XRD confirmed a fully recrystallized single-phase FCC γ matrix with Σ3 twin boundaries and no B2 phase; average grain sizes excluding twins range from 7.60 to 8.95 μm, while including twins they drop to 2.2–3.73 μm. TEM revealed nanosized LRO domains in Cr-rich steels (3Cr: 1.75 nm, 2Ni5Cr: 2.14 nm) and κ-carbides in Ni-containing steels (2Ni: 10.69 nm, 2Ni3Cr: 5.46 nm). IFFT images showed LRO domains with ellipsoidal interconnected morphology typical of spinodal decomposition. A hypothesis was proposed that Cr inhibits while Ni promotes κ-carbide/LRO formation. Important figures/tables: Fig. 1 (EBSD/TEM), Fig. 2 (XRD), Fig. 3 (HRTEM/IFFT), Table 1 (compositions). Subsequent chunks will build on these microstructural features to discuss mechanical properties and deformation behavior.
processing_route: hot rolling to 4 mm with 95% reduction
tensile specimen preparation with gauge 25×3×7 mm³
microstructure: fully recrystallized equiaxed FCC γ with Σ3 twins
grain sizes: 7.6-8.95 μm (excl. twins), 2.2-3.73 μm (incl. twins)
nanosized LRO domains in 3Cr (1.75 nm) and 2Ni5Cr (2.14 nm)
mechanisms: Cr addition reduces κ-carbide/LRO domain size
Ni promotes κ-carbide formation

context_update.summary: This chunk presents the room-temperature tensile properties of the four ultrafast-cooled (UFCed) Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C steels: 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr, and 2Ni5Cr. Engineering stress-strain curves (Fig. 4a) show that total elongation increases with Cr content (2Ni5Cr highest, ~16% increment over 2Ni) while yield strength increases with Ni addition (~141 MPa increase in 2Ni3Cr vs 3Cr). Table 2 provides full tensile data: 2Ni (YS 1202 MPa, TS 1283 MPa, TEL 34.1%), 2Ni3Cr (1110 MPa, 1206 MPa, 45.9%), 2Ni5Cr (1071 MPa, 1187 MPa, 50.5%), 3Cr (969 MPa, 1141 MPa, 54.4%, TS×TEL 62.07 GPa·%). The Ni-Cr alloyed steels exhibit superior strength-ductility synergy compared with other lightweight and austenitic steels, with notably higher yield strength at equivalent TS×TEL (Fig. 4d). True stress-strain and work-hardening rate curves (Fig. 4b,c) reveal a three-stage strain hardening behavior: Stage I rapid decrease, Stage II increase and prolongation with Cr addition, Stage III decrease with higher hardening rates in Cr-rich and Ni-containing steels. Ni addition shortens and lowers Stage II and III. Fractography (Fig. 5) confirms ductile dimple fracture for all four alloys. This chunk provides key mechanical benchmarks for the strengthening mechanism discussions in Sections 4.2 and 4.3.
material_systems: Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C austenitic low-density steel with Ni and Cr additions
key_variables: work hardening stages I, II, III
microstructure: κ-carbide size decreases with Cr (Fig. 3 hypothesis)
LRO domains influenced by Ni and Cr

ultimate tensile strength: 2Ni 1283 MPa; 2Ni3Cr 1206 MPa; 2Ni5Cr 1187 MPa; 3Cr 1141 MPa
strain hardening stages I, II, III with Cr-prolonged Stage II and Cr-elevated Stage III
mechanisms: Cr promotes ductility and prolongs strain hardening Stage II
Ni raises yield strength but shortens Stage II and III
ductile dimple fracture in all four steels
figures_or_tables: Table 2 (tensile results for UFCed steels and references)

context_update.summary: This chunk reports fracture surface SEM confirming ductile dimple fracture for all four UFCed steels (Fig. 5) and provides detailed TEM deformation substructure evolution for 3Cr and 2Ni at various engineering strains (3%, 5%, 10%, near fracture). In 3Cr, dislocation slip with strong planarity, HDDA, and DSBR dominate; deformation twins appear only near fracture and contribute little to ductility. In 2Ni, planar slip bands multiply with three active slip systems, no deformation twins form, and dislocations cut κ-carbides; strain hardening benefits from DSBR. First-principles DFT calculations (Table 3, Fig. 9) quantify formation energies: (Fe,Mn)₃AlC -0.160 eV/atom, Cr₂₁(FeC₃)₂ -0.049 eV/atom, AlNi₃C -0.181 eV/atom, Fe₂NiC 0.082 eV/atom. Cr increases the κ-carbide formation energy and nucleation barrier, suppressing precipitation; Ni reduces the formation energy and promotes κ-carbide formation. Key figures: Fig. 5–9, Table 3.
key_variables: atomic radii: Ni 1.24 Å, Fe 1.27 Å, Mn 1.32 Å
microstructure: κ-carbides cut by dislocation plane slip in 2Ni
mechanisms: HDDA densification and DSBR as primary strain-hardening mechanisms
Cr raises κ-carbide formation energy and nucleation barrier, suppressing κ-carbide size
Ni lowers κ-carbide formation energy via AlNi₃C and promotes κ-carbide growth
dislocation plane slip cuts through κ-carbides in 2Ni

Key variables: Ni content; Cr content; ultra-fast cooling; cumulative strain; Ni content (0, 2 wt%); Cr content (0, 3, 5 wt%); cooling rate ~40 °C/s; cumulative tensile strain
Microstructure: nanoscale κ-carbides; LRO domains; high-density dislocation arrays (HDDA); dynamic slip bands (DSBR); grain structure; fully recrystallized equiaxed FCC γ with Σ3 twins; grain sizes: 7.6-8.95 μm (excl. twins), 2.2-3.73 μm (incl. twins); nanosized LRO domains in 3Cr (1.75 nm) and 2Ni5Cr (2.14 nm)
Properties: density <6.8 g/cm³; YS >1100 MPa; UTS >1200 MPa; TEL >45%; strain hardenability; yield strength: 2Ni 1202 MPa; 2Ni3Cr 1110 MPa; 2Ni5Cr 1071 MPa; 3Cr 969 MPa; ultimate tensile strength: 2Ni 1283 MPa; 2Ni3Cr 1206 MPa; 2Ni5Cr 1187 MPa; 3Cr 1141 MPa; total elongation: 2Ni 34.1%; 2Ni3Cr 45.9%; 2Ni5Cr 50.5%; 3Cr 54.4%
Mechanisms: precipitation strengthening from κ-carbides; solid solution hardening; grain boundary reinforcement; secondary slip activation; HDDA and DSBR by Cr; Cr addition reduces κ-carbide/LRO domain size; Ni promotes κ-carbide formation; Cr promotes ductility and prolongs strain hardening Stage II

context_update.summary: This chunk presents the quantitative yield strength decomposition for the 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr, and 2Ni5Cr steels. The total strength is modeled as σys = σ₀ + σss + σgb + σprep, with σ₀ = 42 MPa from friction stress, σgb determined by Hall-Petch (KHP=446.8 MPa·μm⁰·⁵, effective grain sizes 3.51–2.2 μm, giving 238.5–301.2 MPa), solid solution strengthening σss = 279[C]−1.5[Mn]+20.5[Al]+2.9[Ni]+3.9[Cr] (values 509.5–557.2 MPa), and precipitation strengthening σprep from shearable κ-carbides. The shearing contribution is taken as the larger of order strengthening (σorder) and coherency+modulus mismatch (σcoh+σmod). For LRO-nanodomain-containing 3Cr and 2Ni5Cr, σorder also contributes because some domains are below the critical size. Figure 10 plots σprep vs. precipitate radius for each alloy, revealing the transition radius. Figure 11 shows excellent agreement between the summed calculated strengths and experimental tensile yield strengths. Solid solution is the primary strengthening mechanism; precipitation hardening increases gradually with Ni-Cr synergistic alloying due to increased precipitate size. This section provides quantitative foundation for the subsequent discussion on how Cr and Ni jointly enhance strength-ductility synergy.
material_systems: Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C austenitic steel with Ni and Cr additions
key_variables: Shear modulus G = 70 GPa, ΔG ≈ 23.3 GPa
microstructure: equiaxed recrystallized γ grains with annealing twins
grain sizes incl. twins: 3.51 μm (3Cr), 3.73 μm (2Ni), 3.72 μm (2Ni3Cr), 2.2 μm (2Ni5Cr)
properties: σprep: see Fig. 10; contributions from σorder and σcoh+σmod
mechanisms: solid solution strengthening primary (large C content)
precipitation strengthening via dislocation shearing of κ-carbides
σprep dominated by σcoh+σmod except LRO domains where σorder contributes
synergistic Ni-Cr alloying increases precipitate size and gradual precipitation strengthening
figures_or_tables: Figure 10: precipitation hardening vs. precipitate radius for 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr, 2Ni5Cr
Figure 11: stacked bar chart comparing calculated and experimental yield strengths
Table (unnumbered in chunk): formation energies of κ-carbides and alternative carbides

context_update.summary: Section 4.3 and Conclusions. This chunk describes the evolution of deformation substructures with increasing strain for 3Cr and 2Ni steels, supported by Fig. 12 and a table of strain hardening rates. For 3Cr, initial planar slip is followed by the appearance and proliferation of high-density dislocation arrays (HDDA) in stage II, and in stage III by dislocation annihilation, cross-slip, and slip band refinement; stacking faults and deformation twins appear only near failure. 2Ni steel is dominated by plane slip with uniformly distributed slip bands whose spacing decreases, leading to a shortened strain hardening stage; stage II hardening rate increases, stage III involves dynamic recovery, with scarce stacking faults at failure. Cr alloying promotes dynamic slip band refinement (DSBR) and HDDA, enhancing elongation. The Conclusions restate the superior performance of 2Ni3Cr (YS>1100 MPa, UTS>1200 MPa, TEL>45%), the opposing effects of Ni (lowers nucleation barrier) and Cr (raises it) on κ-carbide precipitation, and the synergy of grain boundary, solid solution, and precipitation strengthening combined with repeated HDDA/DSBR formation. Key figures: Fig. 11 (strength calculation), Fig. 12 (deformation schematics), and strain hardening data table. Future chunks should retain the role of Cr in DSBR/HDDA and the 2Ni3Cr performance benchmark.
properties: strain hardening rate evolution for 3Cr and 2Ni
mechanisms: planar slip constraint
HDDA proliferation
dislocation annihilation and cross-slip
DSBR
Ni reduces nucleation barrier of κ-carbides
Cr elevates nucleation barrier
grain boundary, solid solution, and precipitation strengthening synergy
HDDA and DSBR repeat formation
